keyIdeas: A compound's formula is determined by balancing the charges of its constituent ions so the overall charge is zero.
Common cations to know: Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺, Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺, Zn²⁺, NH₄⁺.
Common anions to know: Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻, O²⁻, OH⁻, NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, CO₃²⁻, PO₄³⁻.
Oxidation numbers are used in IUPAC names to distinguish between multiple oxidation states of the same element: iron(II) = Fe²⁺; iron(III) = Fe³⁺.
To derive a formula from charges: use the 'cross-charge' method — the charge of each ion becomes the subscript of the other, then simplify to the lowest whole-number ratio.
Formulae for polyatomic ions must be enclosed in brackets when the subscript > 1: Ca(NO₃)₂ not CaNO₃₂.

mustKnow.definition: An atom or group of atoms that has gained or lost one or more electrons, giving it a non-zero overall charge.
A Roman numeral in brackets after the element name indicating its oxidation state; used when the element can exist in multiple states (e.g. iron(II), iron(III)).

markSchemePhrases: correct formula with brackets for polyatomic ions
Roman numeral = oxidation state in naming
commandWords: 'Write' the formula of a compound from named ions — use the cross-charge method and simplify. 'Name' a compound whose formula is given — state each ion's name and include the Roman numeral for transition metal oxidation states. 'State' the charge on an ion.
examinerTips: Always use brackets with a subscript for polyatomic ions: Mg(OH)₂ not MgOH₂.
Remember that the formula must give an overall charge of zero — a quick check after writing the formula.
For naming, the Roman numeral equals the oxidation number you calculate: FeCl₃ has Fe³⁺ so it is iron(III) chloride.
Ammonium (NH₄⁺) is the only common cation that is a polyatomic ion — remember the + charge.
Simplify formulae: Ca₂O₂ simplifies to CaO (ratio 1:1).
workedExample:
Write the formula of aluminium sulfate.

Ions: Al³⁺ and SO₄²⁻
Cross-charge: subscript of Al = 2 (charge of sulfate); subscript of SO₄ = 3 (charge of Al).
Formula: Al₂(SO₄)₃

Check: charge = 2(+3) + 3(−2) = +6 − 6 = 0. Correct.
commonTraps: Writing MgOH₂ instead of Mg(OH)₂ — brackets are essential when the subscript applies to a polyatomic ion.
Forgetting to simplify the ratio: Ca₂(SO₄)₂ should be CaSO₄.
Writing the Roman numeral for elements that have only one common oxidation state (e.g. 'sodium(I)' is never written — Na is always +1).
Giving the charge of SO₄ as −1 instead of −2.
